parts.content: click to change
The Oregon Trail
Independence, Missouri  ·  1848

It is 1848. Gold is discovered in California, but you and your family have set your sights on the rich farmland of Oregon's Willamette Valley — 2,000 miles of prairie, river and mountain away.

Stock your wagon wisely, choose your pace, and try to reach Oregon before the winter snows close the mountain passes.
About The Oregon Trail

The Oregon Trail is one of the most beloved educational games ever made. First written in 1971 by student teachers in Minnesota and published for the Apple II by MECC, it taught a generation of schoolchildren about the hardships of 19th-century pioneer life — supply planning, geography, and hard trade-offs — while they tried, and very often failed, to reach Oregon alive.

This is a faithful re-creation for the Classicy desktop, written entirely as a portable HyperCard JSON stack: no code, just cards, buttons, and declarative actions. Outfit your wagon, then manage food, health and money across the trail. Every outcome here is determined by your own choices and supplies — plan well and you will arrive.

Good luck. And try not to die of dysentery.
Who are you?

Your profession sets how much money you start with — and how hard the journey scores. A wealthy banker travels in comfort but earns the fewest points; a farmer starts with almost nothing, yet a farmer who reaches Oregon is honored most of all.
Score multiplier:  Banker ×1     Carpenter ×2     Farmer ×3
When will you set out?
Timing is everything. Leave too early and the spring rivers run high and dangerous from snowmelt. Leave too late and you risk being trapped by snow in the mountains. Seasoned guides recommend May.

parts.content: Matt's General Store — Independence, Missouri
Buy a yoke of oxen (you need at least one to pull the wagon), plenty of food, warm clothing for the mountains, ammunition for hunting, and spare parts for breakdowns.

You'll starve with no food. Buy some provisions first.

else.message: The wagon is loaded and the oxen are yoked. Westward, ho!

parts.content: The Kansas River Crossing

You reach the The Kansas River Crossing. The Kansas River is wide and muddy, swollen with the season's runoff.

How will you get the wagon across?

then.message: The water is low and slow. You ford the river without trouble.

else.message: The current is deep and swift! The wagon nearly tips and supplies wash downstream.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.
You seal the wagon bed with pitch and float across smoothly.

else.message: Water seeps in and some food spoils, but you reach the far bank.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.
You pay the ferryman $20. Safe and dry — though you wait a few days for your turn.
The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

else.message: You can't afford the $20 ferry fee.

onMouseUp.message: You make camp and wait. After several days the river drops, and you ford it safely.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

parts.content: Fort Kearny, Nebraska Territory
You roll into Fort Kearny beside the Platte River — a welcome sight after the open prairie. Here you can rest, hunt buffalo, or press on along the Great Platte River Road.

then.message: You spend a box of ammunition and bring back about 110 lbs of meat.

onOpenCard.message: Chimney Rock towers 300 feet over the plains — a famous landmark, meaning you are making progress. Then… CRACK! A wagon wheel splinters on the rocky ground.

then.message: Luckily you carry a spare. You fit the new wheel and roll on.

else.message: With no spare part, you lose precious days improvising a repair from scrap.

parts.content: Chimney Rock

The great stone spire of Chimney Rock marks the end of the plains and the start of harder country ahead. Emigrants for decades carved their names in the soft rock at its base.

Beyond lies Fort Laramie, the last real chance to resupply before the mountains.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

parts.content: Fort Laramie, Wyoming Territory
Fort Laramie is the last major outpost before the Rockies. Traders, trappers and other emigrant families crowd the stockade. Rest, hunt, trade for supplies — then face the climb.

then.message: You spend a box of ammunition and bring back about 110 lbs of meat.

parts.content: Independence Rock

Pioneers aimed to reach Independence Rock by the Fourth of July — if you were here by Independence Day, you were on schedule to clear the mountains before the snows.

The great granite dome is covered in the painted and carved names of thousands who passed before you. Ahead: the gentle climb to South Pass, the gateway through the Continental Divide.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

parts.content: South Pass & the Parting of the Ways

You cross the Continental Divide at South Pass — so broad and gentle you can barely tell you've reached the top of the Rockies. Soon the trail forks.

The Sublette Cutoff runs straight west: it saves days, but crosses fifty waterless miles of desert. The longer road south by Fort Bridger is slower but safer, with a chance to resupply.

onMouseUp.message: You strike out across the dry cutoff. It is hard on the party and the oxen, but you save many miles.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.
You detour to Fort Bridger. Jim Bridger himself trades you fresh supplies, and the party rests in safety.

else.message: You detour to Fort Bridger. The safer road costs you time, but the party stays whole.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

parts.content: The Snake River Crossing

You reach the The Snake River Crossing. The Snake River runs cold, fast and deep through a rocky canyon — one of the most feared crossings on the whole trail.

How will you get the wagon across?

then.message: The water is low and slow. You ford the river without trouble.

else.message: The current is deep and swift! The wagon nearly tips and supplies wash downstream.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.
You seal the wagon bed with pitch and float across smoothly.

else.message: Water seeps in and some food spoils, but you reach the far bank.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.
Early snow blankets the Blue Mountains. Without enough warm clothing, the party suffers badly in the cold.

else.message: Early snow dusts the Blue Mountains, but your party is well clothed and pushes through the passes.

parts.content: The Blue Mountains

The forested Blue Mountains are the last great climb before the Columbia. The air turns thin and cold, and the timbered slopes are steep and slow going for the oxen.

One final stretch remains: the descent to The Dalles, and the last decision of the journey.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

parts.content: The Dalles — the last choice

You reach The Dalles on the Columbia River. The Willamette Valley is close now, but the Cascade Mountains block the way. There are two ways down.

You can float the wagon down the wild Columbia on a raft — fast, free, and dangerous. Or pay the toll on the new Barlow Road around Mount Hood — slower and costly, but far safer.

then.message: Steady hands and a sound wagon see you through the rapids of the Columbia gorge.

else.message: The rapids catch the raft! You lose supplies to the river and the party is battered and soaked.

then.message: The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.
You pay the $30 toll and take the mountain road around Mount Hood — long, but safe.
The food is gone. Your party goes hungry and grows weak.

else.message: You can't afford the $30 toll. The only way left is to float the river.

parts.content: You have reached Oregon!

The wagon crests the last rise and there it is — the green, river-fed farmland of the Willamette Valley spreading out below. After 2,000 miles of prairie, desert, river and mountain, your family has made it to Oregon.

You stake your claim, and a new life begins.

onOpenCard.value: "You made it " & miles & " miles in " & day & " days before the trail claimed your party."

parts.content: You have died on the trail.

The hardships of the trail were too great. Sickness, hunger and hard miles have ended your journey short of Oregon.

You would not be the first — of the thousands who set out on the Oregon Trail, many never arrived. Rest here, and perhaps another family will fare better.
